generations.text:
**[Sealed with a wax stamp bearing the crest of the Lighthouse of Elmsworth]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the sea roars as though it would devour the very rocks upon which we stand. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as this storm gathers strength, I find myself unable to let go of the words that have long been buried in my soul.

You were once my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the beacon, but the light of my life. I remember the day you were born, the way the sea seemed to hush its voice and the stars seemed to lean closer to the window. You were a child of the tides, and I, your keeper, vowed to guard you from the storm.

But time, like the sea, has a way of eroding the shore. You left, as the waves leave the beach, and I, foolishly, let you go. I told myself it was for your own good, that the world beyond the lighthouse was too cruel, too vast, and that I would protect you from it. But I was wrong, Eleanor. I was wrong to think I could keep you safe from the world. I was wrong to think I could keep you from the pain.

I know the stories they tell of me now—how I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the same bitter cold and unyielding silence that has grown between us. Five years have passed since you left, and yet the weight of your absence still presses upon me like the heavy sea on the rocks below. I have not written to you in that time, nor have I dared to write to you at all. But tonight—tonight, the storm is coming, and I fear it may be the end of me.

You remember the lighthouse, don't you? The one that stands lonely upon the cliffs, a sentinel against the wild and ever-changing sea. It has been my home, my duty, my burden. You used to call it "the old beast" when you visited as a child. I do not know why I allowed you to come, for I knew you would not stay. You were always too quick to run, too eager to leave behind the things that mattered.

I have watched the sea for many years now, and I have learned to read its moods as one might read the pages of a book. Tonight, it is angry. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that makes my hands tremble. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though I have not felt the need to for many a night. The light has been seen, I suppose, and the

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old tower. The sea roars with a fury I have not seen in decades, and the waves crash against the rocks with a vengeance that makes my bones ache. I have kept this lighthouse lit for thirty years, and never before have I felt such a weight upon my soul as I do now, knowing that the storm may claim me before the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five long, bitter years. I have not heard from you, not a single letter, not a word. I have tried to forgive you, as any man of honor must, but the silence between us has grown like a shadow, long and unrelenting. I have tried to believe that you were simply lost, as so many are in this cruel world. But I fear that you were never lost at all—only gone.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness. You know this. I have stood alone here, night after night, with only the light and the sea for company. And yet, in the stillness, I have heard your voice. I have seen your face in the mist, in the glow of the beacon, in the quiet moments when the world is still. I have missed you more than words can say.

I have no illusions, Eliza. I know that you left because

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches with the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since the last time our eyes met, and yet the silence between us feels heavier than any storm that ever raged across these cliffs.

I have kept the lighthouse for as long as I can remember, and though the sea has always been my companion, it is you—my daughter—who has been the truest storm in my life. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried to reach you, to speak to you, to make you understand the love that has never wavered, even when the world seemed to have turned its back on us.

Tonight, the wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as if it knows the end is near. I have seen such storms before, and I know the truth: they do not last forever, but the silence between us may never end. I have no illusions of being able to change the past, nor do I believe that time will mend what has been broken. But I do believe that if I can speak now, before the night swallows me whole, perhaps you will hear me.

I have never stopped thinking of you, Eliza. I have watched the sun rise and set over the ocean, and in every wave, I have seen your face. I have carved your name into the stone of the tower, though I know you will

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know the chances of it doing so are slim. I have written it in the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls. The sea is restless tonight, and the storm is gathering strength—like a storm in a man’s heart, only more cruel.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left this house, this life, and this father behind you. I have tried to forget you, to move on, to let the sea take the weight of my regrets. But the waves have always brought me back to the same place, to the same silence.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, I have not stopped thinking of you. Every night, I sit by the light, and I wonder if you ever think of me. Do you remember the nights we spent by the fire, listening to the sea and talking of the future? I remember the way you would look at the stars, as if they held answers to the questions we never asked. You were always so full of dreams, Eleanor. I used to think that was a blessing. Now I wonder if it was a curse.

This storm is unlike any I have seen in my time. The sky is black as pitch, and the wind screams like a banshee. I fear for my life, but more than that, I fear for the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though I have kept the lighthouse, I have kept my thoughts locked away too—like the lantern, always burning but never letting its light reach the shore.

You were once the light in my life, my little star, and now I find myself lost in the dark, both in spirit and in this cruel sea. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than just my life. I have seen the sky turn to ash, and the waves rise like a beast awakened. I have no doubt that tonight will be the night the sea claims me, and I have no one to tell of the last thing I saw before the dark swallowed me whole.

I have thought of you often, Eleanor. Not in anger, but in sorrow. I have watched the tides and wondered if you still remember the sound of the waves, the way they whispered secrets to the rocks. I have dreamed of you, of the days when you would run up the stairs to see the lantern light, and your eyes would gleam with wonder. You used to call me "Papa" and I would smile, thinking how the world had given me the greatest blessing.

But time is a cruel thing, and the world moves on. I have tried to write to you, to explain, to apologize, but I fear my words have never

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from age, but from the weight of the storm that howls beyond these walls. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. I have not seen the stars in days, and the lighthouse—my life’s work, my burden, my solace—now seems more like a tomb than a beacon.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of letters that went unanswered, of nights where I would sit by the light and wonder if you ever thought of me at all. I have not spoken to you in years, and yet the silence between us is louder than any voice.

I do not know if I will survive this night. The sea has been restless, and the wind has grown fierce. I have seen the sea rise higher than it ever has, and the waves have taken on a life of their own. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every night since you left, but tonight, I fear it may not be enough.

You were my light once, Eleanor. The light that guided me through the darkest nights, the warmth that kept me from despair. I remember the day you left—your eyes were filled with fire, and your voice was sharp with words I could not bear to hear. You said I had become a man of stone, and I let you believe

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart full of both sorrow and longing. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and though the days have passed, the weight of our silence has only grown heavier. I have not spoken to you in so long, and yet, tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself unable to hold back the words that have been buried deep within me.

You were once my light, my greatest hope. I remember the day you left—your face pale, your eyes filled with a sorrow I could not understand. You said you needed to find your own path, that you could not stay where you were. I did not argue, for I feared that if I did, you might not leave at all. But I never imagined that you would not return.

I have kept the lighthouse as you once asked me to, and though I have been alone for years, I have not forgotten the promises we made. I have lit the beacon every night, not just for the sailors who rely on it, but for you, for the daughter I once held close. I have thought of you in every calm breeze, in every storm that threatened to swallow the sea whole.

Tonight, however, the storm is unlike any I have seen. The winds howl like wolves, and the sea roars as if it seeks to reclaim the shore. I fear that this may be the night I do

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a quill that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since you last set foot in this house, and five years since I last heard your voice. The sea has been a faithful companion, but it has never been as kind to me as you once were.

The wind howls now, as it has done for many a night, and the waves lash against the cliffs with a fury that mirrors the tempest within me. Tonight, a storm is coming—one that may not spare the lighthouse, nor the man who guards it. I have seen such storms before, and I know their cruelty. But I have also seen their mercy, and I pray that this time, the sea will be kind.

I have not written to you, Eliza, because I feared what I might say. Words, once spoken, cannot be unsaid. And yet, I have thought of you often, in the quiet hours when the world is still and the only sound is the ticking of the clock and the whisper of the tide. I have imagined your face, your laughter, the way your eyes would light up when you saw me. But you are not here, and I am not the man I once was.

I know I have failed you, and I know I have let you down. I was too proud to ask for your forgiveness, too stubborn to reach out. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though the miles between us may have grown, the ache in my chest grows wider still. I have not written to you, nor have I seen you, and yet I have never ceased to think of you. The sea, my dear, is a cruel and fickle mistress, and so too is the silence that has settled between us.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The sky is black as ink, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for every storm since the day you were born. It is my duty, my solemn vow, to guide those who sail the waters beyond. But tonight, I fear the sea may take more than it gives. I have seen the waves rise higher than they ever have, and I do not know if the light will be enough to keep the darkness at bay—or if I will be there to see the dawn.

I have no illusions, Eleanor. I know you have moved on, and perhaps you have found peace where I have not. You were never one to stay in the same place, and I must admit, I have not been the easiest of fathers. I was stubborn, set in my ways, and too proud to admit that I had wronged you. You were a child then, and I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the spirits of the sea that once claimed our family. The storm is upon us, and I fear the light may not hold long enough to guide the ships through the night. I have not seen you in five years, and I do not know if I will live to see you again. But if I do, I hope this letter finds you.

I have kept your portrait on the wall of the lighthouse, though it has grown dim with time and the salt of the sea. You were a fine girl, Eleanor. Too fine, perhaps. You were always so proud, so certain of your path. I tried to understand, to follow, but I was a man of the sea, not of the city. You wanted to be a doctor, to save lives, not to stand in the cold and watch the waves. You left without a word, and I have not heard from you since.

I do not blame you for leaving. I only regret that I did not understand you better. I was too stubborn, too set in my ways. I thought I was protecting you, but I only held you back. I should have let you go, and I should have let you be.

The lighthouse is my life, my duty, my burden. I have spent years keeping the light burning, even when the world outside was dark. I have seen the sea rage, the ships sink,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The sea howls in the distance, as if it too mourns the silence between us. The storm is upon us, and I fear the wind may carry my voice away before I can reach you.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of watching the dawn over the cliffs, of tending to the lamp that guides the lost, and of listening to the sea whisper your name. I have not spoken of you to a soul, not even to the old Captain who once called me "old man" with a grin. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves grow wilder, I feel the need to say something—anything—to you.

You were not the first to leave. I have known loss before, but none like the hollow ache of your absence. You were my light, my only light, and now it is gone. I have tried to carry on, to be the steadfast lighthouse keeper you once thought I was. But I have not been. I have been a man who forgot how to love, who let the world wear him down like the tide wears the rocks.

I know you must think me cruel, unfeeling, a man who turned his back on you. But I was not ready. I was not ready to let you go, nor to let you see me as I truly am—broken

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since your last letter, and I find myself writing now with a heart full of regret and a mind clouded by the weight of silence. The sea has been restless these past few days, and I fear the storm that approaches may be the end of me. But before I go, I must speak, for I have kept this secret too long.

I know you believe I abandoned you. That I chose the light over the love of my child. But I must tell you the truth, as the wind howls through the tower and the waves claw at the shore like the ghosts of my past.

You were five when I left. I had no choice. The light must always burn, even when the world is dark. I was the only one who could keep the beacon lit, and I swore to protect this place, this duty, this life. I thought it was for you, Eleanor. I thought by keeping the light alive, I was keeping you safe. I thought I was doing the right thing.

But I was wrong. I was selfish. I let my duty become my excuse. I let fear of the unknown cloud my judgment. I let the sea take me away from you, and I never once thought to come back.

I have not forgotten you, my dear. I have dreamed of you every night, of your laughter, of your small hands clutching mine as we walked along the shore. I have carried

**April 12th, 1897**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse. The sea is restless, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and grey. I fear the storm will not be kind to us. But I must write, for I do not know if I will live to see another dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years. I have not heard from you, not a single word. I have not seen your face, nor heard your voice. You were my only child, my light in the darkness, and now you are a shadow I cannot reach.

I know you do not want to hear from me. I know you have your own life, your own family, your own regrets. But I write not to beg for forgiveness, nor to demand your return. I write because I am alone, and I need to speak, even if it is to a ghost.

You were always a clever girl, Eleanor. I remember the way you would sit by the window at night, sketching the sea and the stars, your eyes full of wonder. You had a gift for seeing what others could not. I used to tell you that the lighthouse was not just a tower of stone and light, but a place where the world met the sky, and where the soul could

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not only from the cold but from the weight of years unspoken. The sea howls outside, a voice like a thousand mourners, and I find myself recalling the last time I heard your voice—faint, distant, as if carried by the very wind that now threatens to swallow us whole.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left. I do not know what drove you to walk away, but I have spent every day since trying to understand. I have not written, nor have I called. I have not even dared to think of you, for fear that the thought would bring more pain than the silence.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself unable to remain still. The lighthouse stands tall, its light still burning, though I fear it may not be enough to guide the ships through this maelstrom. I have always believed in the power of light—both in the sea and in the soul. And yet, I cannot help but wonder if I have been the one who has cast shadows where there should have been light.

I have no regrets, not for the choices I made, nor for the ones I did not. But I do regret that you were not there to see the lighthouse through its darkest hours. I regret that you did not see the man I have tried to be—though I fear you may have seen only the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time I laid eyes upon you, and though the world turns on, I have not forgotten the day you left. You were but a girl then, and I, a man worn by time and duty. I had hoped, perhaps, that the sea would carry your words to me, or that the wind would whisper your name through the stones of this lonely keep. But no, the sea is silent now, and the wind has long since turned its back on me.

Tonight, a storm is coming—one that promises to be unlike any I have seen in my years here. The sky is dark as ink, and the waves grow restless, as if they too sense the reckoning that awaits. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and though it burns bright, I fear it may not be enough to guide the lost or the damned. I have no doubt that I will not see the dawn.

And so, I write this letter—not to beg for your forgiveness, for I do not deserve it, nor to plead for your return, for I know the wound you left behind is too deep to mend. But I write it because I have no one else to tell you the truth, and because, in this final hour, I must say what I have always wanted to say.

You were never the daughter I wished

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the flicker of a dying lamp in my hand, the sea howling like a wounded beast outside the keep. The wind has been rising all evening, and the waves have grown bold, daring to crash against the cliffs with a fury I’ve not seen in years. I pray the storm passes before the dawn, but I fear it may not.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of a heart that has grown heavy with the weight of your absence. I’ve tried to write to you, but the words always seemed to fall short—too clumsy, too full of regret, too much like a man who has lost his way.

I’ve been a lighthouse keeper for nearly thirty years now, and I’ve seen many storms. Some came with warnings, others came unbidden, and a few… well, a few claimed lives. I’ve always believed that the light must burn through the darkest night. That is my duty, my purpose. But tonight, I fear the light may be extinguished before its time.

You were my light once, Eleanor. The first time I saw you, standing at the edge of the cliffs with your father, you were a vision of hope and promise. You were the reason I took the post, the reason I stayed when the world turned its back on me. I wanted to be a father to you, to guide you, to teach you the

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a hand that trembles—not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken, of time lost, and of the storm that now roils beyond the cliffs, threatening to swallow the lighthouse whole. You may not believe me, but I do not write this to beg your forgiveness, nor to plead for your return. I write this because I am a man who has spent his life watching the sea, and I know that when the night is darkest, the stars are still there, waiting to be seen.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of dreams that ended before they could begin. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried to reach you. I have sent letters, and I have sent them again, each time with the hope that one day, you might read them. But you never did. And I do not know if you ever will.

You were always the child of the sea, I think. Not the sea itself, but the wind that dances upon its surface, the storm that cannot be tamed. You were never meant to be tamed, and I was never meant to be the one to hold you back. Perhaps that is why I let you go.

But now, as the sky darkens and the wind howls like a beast unleashed, I find myself wondering if I have made a mistake. I have spent my life ensuring the light

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since I last set eyes upon you, and I find myself writing this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the stone that anchors this lighthouse. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that seems to echo the storm of my own soul. Tonight, the sky is dark as ink, and the waves rise like vengeful giants, crashing against the cliffs with a sound that might as well be the echo of your last words to me.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Not a single word. I have not written, nor have I sent a letter. I have not even dared to dream of you, for fear that such dreams would bring me closer to the truth of what I have done. But now, as I stand alone on this wind-swept tower, with the light flickering like a dying ember, I feel the weight of my silence pressing upon me like the tide.

I know you blame me for the way things turned out. You think I abandoned you. But I did not. I have never stopped thinking of you. I have never stopped waiting for you to come back. I have kept the light burning, not just for the ships that pass by, but for you, in the hope that one day, you would return and see the light still shining.

You were only ten when you left, and I was a man who

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside this lighthouse, and the sea roars as if it too knows the truth of what I must say. I have not spoken to you in five years, and now, as the storm gathers strength, I fear I may never see you again.

I have always been a man of silence, Eleanor. A man who kept his thoughts locked behind the thick stone walls of this lighthouse, just as I kept my love for you behind a wall of pride and fear. I thought I could protect you by keeping you at a distance. I thought I could spare you the pain of knowing the kind of man I was, the kind of father I had become.

You were my light, Eleanor. The one thing that ever made me feel whole. When you were small, I would sit by the beacon and watch the sea, dreaming of the day you would grow up and see the world. But I was a fool, and I let my pride blind me. I let the weight of this duty, this responsibility, crush the very thing that made me a man.

I remember the night you left. It was a quiet evening, the sea calm, the sky clear. I watched you walk down the path to the village, and I did not stop you. I did not call after you. I told myself it was for your own good

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since you last set foot in this lighthouse, and though the world may have moved on, I have not. I still stand here each night, watching the sea, the sky, and the flickering light that guides the lost and the weary. But tonight, the stars are not my comfort — they are my only witness to this letter, for I fear the storm that approaches may take more than just the sea.

You once called me "Dad," a word that once brought warmth to my soul. Now it feels like a ghost, lingering in the air like the salt that clings to my skin. I have not spoken to you in years, and I do not know if words will ever bridge the chasm between us. But tonight, as the wind howls and the waves grow wilder, I feel the need to speak — not for my own peace, but for the peace of my soul.

I have always believed in the duty of this lighthouse. You know that. I have always believed in the duty of a father to protect, to guide, to be a steady light in the darkness. But I have failed you, Eleanor. I have failed you in more ways than I can name. I let my work consume me, I let my pride blind me, and I let fear keep me from reaching out when you needed me most

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not. I write to you now, on the eve of a storm that threatens to swallow the sea and the sky alike, and in the stillness of this moment, I find myself compelled to speak. Not to you, but to the memory of the girl who once stood at my side, wide-eyed and full of questions, as I lit the beacon for the first time.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not written to you, nor have you written to me. I suppose it is a matter of time, and time, as you well know, is a cruel master. I have not been the same since the night you left, and I fear I have not been the father you deserved.

The lighthouse has been my world these many years. The sea, the wind, the endless horizon—it has kept me company when the silence of your absence grew too heavy to bear. I have kept the lamp burning, as I always have, though now it feels like a hollow gesture. A beacon for the ships that no longer sail the waters I once watched with pride. A light for the world, but not for me.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The sky is a swirling mass of black and silver, and the waves rise like the hands of the dead, clawing at the rocks below. I have seen such storms before, and I have survived them.

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**

*To my dear, lost daughter,*

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has long since stopped beating in time with the world. The sea, as ever, is restless tonight. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that makes me fear for my life. I have spent fifty years in this lighthouse, and never before has the night felt so heavy, so full of meaning. It is as if the sea itself is whispering to me, telling me it is time.

You know I have not written to you in five years. I have not spoken to you, not even in letters, and I do not know if you will ever read this. But tonight, as the storm gathers, I feel the need to say something—anything—that might reach you, even if it is too late.

You were a child when you left. A child who did not understand the weight of the world, or the burden of a man who must always be watching, always on guard, always alone. I tried to be a father to you, but I was not always there. I was too busy keeping the light burning, too busy ensuring that no ship would be lost to the dark. And in my absence, you grew up, and I did not. I did not know how to be a father, and I did not know how to be a man.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart that aches like the wind howling through the cracks in this old lighthouse. Five years have passed since you left, and though the days have worn on, the weight of your absence has never truly lifted. I have not spoken to you in all that time, and I fear I may never again.

The sea has always been my constant companion, the ever-watchful guardian of these cliffs. But tonight, it is different. A storm is coming—one that does not merely stir the waves, but seems to rage against the very soul of the earth. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and silver. I have seen such storms before, but never one that feels so... personal.

I have been watching the horizon for hours, my lamp burning with a steady, unwavering light. It is my duty, you understand, and it is my penance. I have always believed that the sea must be guided, that the light must shine, even in the darkest hours. But I wonder, now, if I have been guiding you as well, or if I have only been trying to keep you from seeing the truth.

You were so young when you left, Eleanor. A child still, with eyes full of fire and a heart full of dreams. You told me you needed to go, that you could not stay here, that you

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs we call home. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself longing for your voice more than ever.

I have not written to you since the day you left. I was too proud, too stubborn, too afraid to reach out. I thought that if I stayed silent, the pain would fade. But the silence has only grown louder, and I have spent these years haunted by the echoes of your last words.

I know you left because of me. I know you thought I had betrayed you. I know you believed I had chosen duty over family. But I had no choice, Eliza. The light must shine, and the sea must be watched. I was your father, and I swore to protect this lighthouse, this place, this life. I did not mean to break your heart.

**The Lighthouse, Cape Dune**  
**April 5th, 1897**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old tower. The sea has grown restless, and the sky is bruised with the weight of an oncoming storm. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and the light cuts through the darkness like a blade. But tonight, I feel it is not just the sea that threatens me—no, it is the silence, the weight of the years that have passed since you left.

I have not written to you, not because I do not care, but because I feared the words would not be enough. I feared that even if I spoke, you would not listen.

**The Lighthouse, Black崖 Point**  
**April 13th, 1892**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned, and the sea roaring as though it seeks to swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the waves crash against the cliffs and the beacon flickers in the dark, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in a lifetime.  

You were always a fire, Eliza—bright, fierce, and unrelenting. I remember the day you left, the way you stood at the door of our cottage, your eyes like flint, your voice like ice. "You never wanted me," you said, and I didn’t answer. I couldn’t. I had spent years trying to be the kind of father you deserved, but I was blind to the truth. I was a man who saw only the sea, and not the girl who grew up in its shadow.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the arms of some man who has promised you a better life than I could ever offer. Five years is a long time to go without a word, and I have not written because I did not know how. Words, you see, are like the sea—fickle, unpredictable, and often cruel. I feared they would not carry the truth, and the truth, I have come to believe, is too heavy to send through the mail.

I have been alone here for a long time, Eliza. The sea has been my companion, the wind my confidant, and the light my purpose.
